name: Auckland
tagline: NZ's biggest city — beaches, islands, sky-high views and a world-class zoo
summary: Auckland is New Zealand's most visited city and a genuine family destination in its own right. With 50+ regional parks, two harbours, ferry trips to Waiheke and Rangitoto, Kelly Tarlton's SEA LIFE Aquarium, Auckland Zoo, and Sky Tower, there's easily a week of family content here — much of it free or low-cost.

faqs.q: How many days do you need in Auckland with kids?
faqs.a: 3 days is comfortable for first-timers — Sky Tower, Kelly Tarlton's, and a Waiheke ferry day. 5 days lets you add Zoo, West Coast beaches, and Tiritiri Matangi Island.

faqs.q: Is Auckland worth visiting with kids?
faqs.a: Yes — Auckland is underrated as a family destination. The aquarium, zoo, ferry trips, and volcanic island walks are genuinely world-class. The city is easy to navigate with kids and has excellent holiday parks close to the beach.

faqs.q: What is the best beach near Auckland for families?
faqs.a: Mission Bay is the most convenient — calm water, playground, cafes. For something wilder, Piha and Muriwai on the West Coast are dramatic black sand beaches. Tawharanui Regional Park (45min north) is stunning and uncrowded mid-week.

faqs.q: Is Kelly Tarlton's worth it for families?
faqs.a: Yes for ages 3–12. The penguin colony and shark tunnel are highlights. Allow 90 minutes and book online to save 15–20% on gate prices.

faqs.q: How many days do we need in Auckland?
faqs.a: Two to three days covers the main city attractions comfortably. Day 1: Auckland Museum (free for NZ residents; fee for international visitors), Cornwall Park, One Tree Hill walk. Day 2: Kelly Tarlton's Sea Life Aquarium, Mission Bay beach, Tamaki Drive drive. Day 3: Auckland Zoo or Rainbow's End, Devonport ferry trip. If you want to include Waiheke Island or Rangitoto, add another day.

faqs.q: Is Waiheke Island worth the ferry with kids?
faqs.a: Yes — Waiheke is 35 minutes by ferry from the CBD and one of the best day trips from Auckland for families. The island has beautiful beaches (Oneroa, Palm Beach), good cycling on e-bikes, excellent cafés, and stunning views. The ferry itself is part of the experience — kids love it. Best in summer when swimming is the goal. The main drag in Oneroa village has good food options. Book return ferries ahead in summer as they fill up.

name: Bay of Islands
tagline: 144 islands, dolphins, sailing, and New Zealand's birthplace — all in one bay
summary: The Bay of Islands is NZ's original holiday destination and still one of the best. Paihia is the main family base — from there you can swim with dolphins, sail to deserted islands, visit the Waitangi Treaty Grounds, and enjoy some of the warmest water in the country.
verdict: Best NZ beach holiday for families. Come in January for the warmest water. Combine with the Kauri Coast.
best_for: Beach-loving families, water activities, NZ history
not_ideal_for: Those wanting to avoid the 3.5-hour drive from Auckland

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Car needed to get there. Once in Paihia, everything is walkable or boat-based.

faqs.q: Can you actually swim with dolphins at the Bay of Islands?
faqs.a: Yes, but it depends on the dolphins cooperating. Most operators run swim/watch tours. Guaranteed sightings but not guaranteed in-water encounters. Book with Explore NZ or Fullers.

faqs.q: How far is Bay of Islands from Auckland?
faqs.a: About 3.5 hours by car via SH1. Most families drive up — no direct train. Flights take 40 mins to Kerikeri.

faqs.q: Is Russell worth visiting?
faqs.a: Yes — take the 10-minute ferry from Paihia ($15 return). Russell is NZ's first European settlement, quiet and historic. Great for lunch.

faqs.q: What is the best base for Bay of Islands — Paihia or Russell?
faqs.a: Paihia is the main hub with most accommodation, restaurants, and tour operators — it's the easier choice for families. Russell is quieter, charming, and reached by ferry (5 min, $14 return for adults, kids free). Russell is worth a half-day visit regardless of where you stay — the Duke of Marlborough is NZ's oldest pub and the beach is lovely. Stay in Paihia for convenience, visit Russell for the character.

faqs.q: Is the Hole in the Rock worth doing with young kids?
faqs.a: The Cape Brett cruise including the Hole in the Rock is a full-day trip (4–5 hours) and involves open water — check conditions before booking, as it can be rough. Kids 7+ usually manage well; younger children are hit-or-miss depending on sea conditions and temperament. The shorter Dolphin Discovery cruise (3 hours) is better for under-6s — more sheltered water and reliable dolphin encounters.

faqs.q: What are the best beaches in Bay of Islands for families?
faqs.a: Waitangi Beach (in front of the Treaty Grounds, patrolled in summer), Paihia Beach (calm, sheltered, central), Haruru Falls reserve and beach (less known, beautiful), and across the water, Long Beach in Russell (one of the best family beaches in Northland). For something special, the offshore islands (Urupukapuka Island) have spectacular snorkelling — accessible by water taxi.

name: Christchurch
tagline: Gateway to the South Island — and surprisingly great for families
summary: Christchurch is the South Island hub and a genuinely underrated family destination. The International Antarctic Centre is world-class, the Gondola has incredible views, and it's one of the most affordable major cities to visit in NZ. Plus it's the base for road trips to Queenstown, Kaikoura and the West Coast.
verdict: Underrated family city. 2 nights is ideal — great base for South Island road trips.
best_for: Families using it as a South Island base, or those wanting an affordable city break
not_ideal_for: Those wanting beach holidays (beaches are cold, not tropical)

faqs.q: Is Christchurch worth visiting after the earthquakes?
faqs.a: Absolutely. The rebuild has created one of NZ's most interesting city centres — street art, container malls, modern architecture alongside heritage buildings. The 'gap filler' culture is unique.

faqs.q: What is Christchurch best known for?
faqs.a: The Antarctic Centre (genuinely world-class), the Gondola, and as the South Island's main hub. Also known for its garden city parks and punting on the Avon River.

faqs.q: Is the Antarctic Centre worth it for kids?
faqs.a: Yes — the International Antarctic Centre is one of the best paid attractions in NZ for families. The Hagglund all-terrain vehicle ride, the storm experience, the Little Blue penguins, and the interactive exhibits are all excellent. Budget 2.5–3 hours. Book online for a small discount. It's particularly good for rainy days and is right next to the airport, making it a perfect first or last activity.

faqs.q: What is free to do in Christchurch with kids?
faqs.a: Several excellent free options: Botanic Gardens and punting (gondola is paid but the gardens are free), Canterbury Museum, Hagley Park, New Brighton beach and pier, the Avon River Precinct, and the Cardboard Cathedral (free to enter). The city centre is compact and very walkable. The free City Circuit bus connects most of the main attractions.

faqs.q: How far is Christchurch from the ski fields?
faqs.a: Mt Hutt is the closest ski field — about 90 minutes from central Christchurch via Methven. It's a large, well-developed family resort with a good ski school for kids. Porters and Craigieburn are closer (60 minutes) but smaller and more suited to intermediate skiers. On the way to Mt Hutt, stop at Methven for gear hire — it's cheaper than buying equipment in Christchurch.

name: Coromandel
tagline: Hot Water Beach, Cathedral Cove, and some of NZ's most beautiful beaches within 2.5 hours of Auckland
summary: The Coromandel Peninsula is Auckland's backyard holiday spot — and for good reason. Hot Water Beach (dig your own hot pool in the sand), Cathedral Cove sea arch, Thames goldfields, and a string of gorgeous beaches make it one of the best NZ family destinations. Best in summer.
verdict: Essential for Auckland families and any North Island road trip. Hot Water Beach alone makes it worth visiting.
best_for: Beach holidays, Auckland day trips or overnights, hot spring lovers
not_ideal_for: Families wanting theme parks or rainy-day activities — it's very outdoor-focused

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Car essential. Fuel up in Thames — petrol is expensive on the peninsula.

faqs.q: When is Hot Water Beach best?
faqs.a: The geothermal seep is only accessible 2 hours either side of low tide. Check hotwatertide.co.nz before you go. Weekday mornings in shoulder season are the least crowded.

faqs.q: Is Cathedral Cove better by boat or on foot?
faqs.a: Boat if you have young kids (avoids the 35-min walk each way). On foot if your kids can manage it — the walk is lovely. The sea arch looks the same either way.

faqs.q: Can you do Coromandel as a day trip from Auckland?
faqs.a: Yes — just about. Hot Water Beach + Cathedral Cove is a very long day (5h driving total). Better to stay 1–2 nights and actually enjoy it.

faqs.q: How far is Hot Water Beach from Auckland?
faqs.a: About 2.5 hours from Auckland via the main state highway, or 3 hours if you take the more scenic Coromandel Town route. It's achievable as a long day trip but overnight is much better — you can dig your hot pool at the optimal low tide time (check tide times before you go — the beach only works within 2 hours either side of low tide). The Coromandel Peninsula is most enjoyable as a 2–3 night trip.

faqs.q: Is Cathedral Cove walkable with kids?
faqs.a: The Cathedral Cove walkway is 3.2km return from the Hahei car park, taking about 1.5 hours return with young kids. The track is well-formed but hilly in places — doable for most kids 5+ with patience. During peak summer (Christmas to late January), cars can't park at the trailhead and a shuttle runs from Hahei. Alternatively, take a water taxi from Hahei (10 minutes) to arrive directly at the cove — far easier with under-5s.

faqs.q: What is there to do in Coromandel besides Hot Water Beach and Cathedral Cove?
faqs.a: Quite a lot: the Coromandel Walkway (if your kids are older, 7km point-to-point), Driving Creek Railway (a narrow-gauge scenic railway — excellent for kids of all ages), Thames Goldmine experience, kayaking from Hahei or Whangamata, and Whangapoua Beach (one of the most beautiful and least-visited beaches on the peninsula). The whole peninsula has excellent fish and chips at local takeaways — a NZ coastal tradition.

name: Dunedin
tagline: Yellow-eyed penguins, albatross, sea lions, and NZ's most dramatic urban wildlife experience
summary: Dunedin is New Zealand's wildlife capital — the Otago Peninsula is the only place in the world where you can see royal albatross, yellow-eyed penguins, and New Zealand sea lions all within an hour of a city centre. It is also a beautiful Victorian city with excellent cafes, a world-class university, and the world's steepest street (Baldwin Street).
verdict: Outstanding for families who love wildlife. The Otago Peninsula wildlife experiences are genuinely world-class and unlike anything else in NZ. Worth a 2-3 day detour for any South Island road trip.
best_for: Families who love wildlife and nature, ages 5+ (best for 8+ for the wildlife tours)
not_ideal_for: Beach holidays or adventure activities — Dunedin is cold and grey much of the year

faqs.q: What age is suitable for the wildlife experiences in Dunedin?
faqs.a: The Royal Albatross Centre tour suits ages 5+ (some waiting in a hide). Yellow-eyed penguin viewing is best for ages 8+ as it involves waiting quietly at dusk — younger kids can find the stillness challenging. Sea lions are free to observe from distance and suit any age. All wildlife experiences require advance booking.

faqs.q: Is yellow-eyed penguin viewing worth the cost?
faqs.a: Yes — yellow-eyed penguins are one of the world's rarest penguins (approximately 4,000 remaining) and Dunedin is the most accessible place in the world to observe them. Penguin Place ($90/adult) provides the most intimate and conservation-focused experience. DOC viewing hides ($30/adult) are cheaper but less guided. Both are genuinely moving experiences that kids remember.

faqs.q: Is Dunedin worth visiting with kids?
faqs.a: Yes — Dunedin is genuinely underrated for families. The Royal Albatross Centre at Taiaroa Head is world-class (only mainland royal albatross colony in the world). The Otago Peninsula wildlife — sea lions, little blue penguins, fur seals — is incredible for kids. Dunedin's Victorian architecture and the famous Baldwin Street (world's steepest street) are quirky attractions. The Otago Settlers Museum and Dunedin Railway Station are both worth a visit.

faqs.q: What is the Otago Peninsula and is it worth the drive?
faqs.a: The Otago Peninsula is a 30km scenic drive from Dunedin — absolutely worth it with kids. Wildlife highlights: royal albatrosses at Taiaroa Head (book the tour at the visitor centre), sea lions at Allans Beach (free, walk along the beach and they're often right there), and little blue penguins at Pilots Beach at dusk (free, return to shore just after sunset — spectacular). Allow a full day for the peninsula.

faqs.q: How many days do we need in Dunedin?
faqs.a: Two days minimum: Day 1 — Dunedin city (Railway Station, Speight's Brewery tour for adults, Otago Museum, Baldwin Street), Day 2 — Otago Peninsula full day (albatrosses, sea lions, penguins at dusk). A third day can include a train trip on the Taieri Gorge Railway (one of NZ's most spectacular rail journeys, 4.5 hours return) or a day trip to Moeraki Boulders (90 min north — remarkable spherical rocks on the beach).

route_gems.name: The Catlins

name: Fiordland & Milford Sound
tagline: The most dramatic scenery in New Zealand — Milford Sound will stop your kids mid-sentence
summary: Fiordland is remote, dramatic, and genuinely unlike anywhere else on Earth. Milford Sound — sheer 1,500m peaks rising from dark water, waterfalls cascading year-round, dolphins surfing the bow wave of your cruise boat — is a once-in-a-lifetime experience. The drive in from Te Anau is as spectacular as the destination.
verdict: Requires a detour and some planning, but Milford Sound is the single most dramatic landscape experience in NZ. Worth every bit of the effort.
best_for: Families with kids 5+ who appreciate epic scenery. Teenagers are consistently blown away.
not_ideal_for: Families needing lots of activities or beach time. Milford Sound itself has limited things to do beyond the cruise.
free_activities: The Milford Sound highway drive itself (SH94) — one of the world's great drives
Mirror Lakes stop (10 min from Te Anau) — perfectly still reflections, boardwalk
Lake Te Anau waterfront and lakeside walk
The Chasm (free, 10-min walk, dramatic gorge — kids love it)
Monkey Creek and Homer Tunnel viewpoints on SH94
hidden_gems: Cruise in the rain — Milford Sound is famous for waterfalls. Rain creates hundreds of extra waterfalls cascading down the cliffs. A wet day is genuinely the best time to cruise.
Book the first cruise of the day (earliest departure) — before the tour buses from Queenstown arrive, Milford has a completely different atmosphere
The Chasm on SH94 is often rushed past — the 10-min return walk to the churning gorge is spectacular and completely free
Te Anau town is the best base — stay here rather than driving to Milford and back from Queenstown in one day (brutal 8h round trip)
Mirror Lakes at golden hour (late afternoon) — the reflection only works on calm days and the light is magical in the evening
Fiordland Cinema in Te Anau plays 'Ata Whenua — Shadowland' (30 min film about Fiordland) for $15 — excellent context before your Milford cruise
rainy_day: Milford Sound cruise (rain = more waterfalls = better experience)
Te Anau Glowworm Caves (underground, weather irrelevant)
Fiordland Cinema (Te Anau)
Te Anau cafes and the waterfront walk

day1.title: Queenstown to Te Anau
activities: Drive Queenstown to Te Anau (2h via SH6)
Lake Te Anau waterfront walk
Te Anau Glowworm Caves (book ahead)
Dinner at The Fat Duck or Sandfly Café

day2.title: Milford Sound Cruise Day
activities: Early start — drive SH94 to Milford (2h, stop at Mirror Lakes and The Chasm)
Morning Milford Sound cruise (book earliest available)
Explore Milford after cruise
Drive back to Te Anau

day3.title: Te Anau + Depart
activities: Kepler Track day section (Lake Te Anau lookout, 3–4h return for fit families)
Or relaxed morning in Te Anau
Drive to Queenstown (2h) or Dunedin (4h 30m)

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Own car essential — no public transport to Milford Sound. Fill up with fuel in Te Anau.

faqs.q: Is Milford Sound better from Queenstown or Te Anau?
faqs.a: Always base in Te Anau. Driving from Queenstown is 4h each way — an exhausting 8h round trip that leaves little time at Milford itself. Te Anau is 2h from Milford and a lovely base.

faqs.q: What's the best Milford Sound cruise?
faqs.a: Real Journeys and Cruise Milford are the main reputable operators. The 2-hour cruise covers the full fiord. The 'nature cruise' with commentary is better for families than the cheaper scenic cruises.

faqs.q: Is the drive to Milford Sound safe with kids?
faqs.a: Yes — SH94 is well-maintained and spectacular. Allow 2h from Te Anau with stops. The Homer Tunnel (1.2km, one lane, traffic lights) is exciting for kids. Check the NZ Transport Agency website for any closures.

faqs.q: Is Milford Sound worth the drive with kids?
faqs.a: Yes — Milford Sound is one of those places that lives up to the hype even with tired kids in the car. The 2-hour drive from Te Anau through the Homer Tunnel is itself spectacular. A 2-hour boat cruise lets you see Mitre Peak, Bowen Falls, and seals. The whole experience (drive in, cruise, drive out) takes a full day from Te Anau. Stay in Te Anau the night before to avoid a very early start. The Milford Road is one of NZ's most scenic drives.

faqs.q: What is the best cruise option for families in Milford Sound?
faqs.a: For families, the 2-hour cruise is the standard choice — long enough to see everything without kids losing interest. Real Journeys and Go Orange both run family-friendly cruises with indoor heated lounges. If seasickness is a concern, choose a larger vessel — the catamaran-style boats are more stable than smaller vessels. Underwater Observatory cruises also visit the unique marine observation chamber — a bonus activity worth adding.

faqs.q: Can I drive to Milford Sound in a campervan?
faqs.a: Yes, but check your campervan rental terms — some companies restrict driving through the Homer Tunnel. The road is sealed all the way and campervans are common. There is a basic DOC campsite at Milford Sound itself (book ahead — it fills fast in summer) and better facilities at Te Anau. Do not attempt the Milford Road in heavy snow or ice without chains. Road conditions are available at the NZTA website.

route_gems.tagline: One of the world's most dramatic road tunnels — carved through sheer mountain, kea parrots hang around outside
route_gems.tip: Kea (NZ mountain parrots) are often seen at the Homer Tunnel portal. Don't let them near your car — they chew rubber seals!

name: Gisborne & East Cape
tagline: World's first sunrise, surf beaches and remote Maori heartland — worth the drive
summary: Gisborne and the East Cape are among New Zealand's most undervisited family destinations — and among the most rewarding. Gisborne itself has excellent surf beaches, a vibrant food scene, and Young Nick's Head. The East Cape road is one of NZ's great scenic drives: lighthouse, pohutukawa-lined beaches, and the real Maori heartland of Tairawhiti. Cell coverage is minimal and petrol stations sparse — which is exactly the point.

quick_facts.rainy_day_options: Limited — this is an outdoor destination
quick_facts.phone_coverage: Good in Gisborne, minimal on East Cape road

top_activities: East Cape Lighthouse — NZ's easternmost point, world's first sunrise
Tolaga Bay Wharf — longest wharf in NZ (660m), great for fishing
Wainui Beach surf lessons (beginner-friendly)
Te Kuri a Paoa (Young Nick's Head) — where Cook first spotted land
Eastwoodhill Arboretum — NZ's largest collection of northern hemisphere trees
Rere Rockslide — natural waterslide in the Wharekopae River

Titirangi Reserve lookout, Gisborne
free_activities: Wainui and Midway Beaches — surf town beaches, free

East Cape road drive — all beaches and marae sites free to drive past
Te Poho-o-Rawiri Marae — Gisborne's large meeting house (tours by arrangement)

hidden_gems: Rere Rockslide — a natural rock water slide in the river, 45min from Gisborne. Bring a boogie board. Genuinely the most fun thing in the region.
Tolaga Bay Wharf — peaceful, historic, perfect for fishing with kids. Longest wharf in NZ.
Anaura Bay — one of NZ's least-visited beautiful bays, DoC campsite on the beach

three_day_itinerary.day1: Gisborne beach, Rere Rockslide, Young Nick's Head lookout
three_day_itinerary.day2: East Cape drive — Tolaga Bay Wharf, Tikitiki church, Hicks Bay overnight
three_day_itinerary.day3: East Cape Lighthouse at sunrise, complete loop back to Gisborne or Opotiki

faqs.q: Is the East Cape drive worth it with kids?
faqs.a: Yes — but only if you're happy off the beaten track. The lighthouse, Tolaga Bay, and Anaura Bay are genuinely beautiful. The road is narrow and mostly unsealed for short stretches. Allow 2 full days for the circuit and stock up on food and petrol in Opotiki or Gisborne before you leave.

faqs.q: What is the Rere Rockslide?
faqs.a: A natural 60m long rock water slide in the Wharekopae River, 45 minutes inland from Gisborne. You bring your own bodyboard or sit-on-board and slide down the smooth limestone. It's free, safe for most ages (river is cold), and one of the most fun free things in NZ.

faqs.q: When does the sun rise first in NZ?
faqs.a: East Cape Lighthouse area is the first place on the NZ mainland to see the sun each day. In summer, sunrise is around 5:30am. Many families drive the Cape and camp near the lighthouse for a sunrise experience. The lighthouse is a 700m walk up a steep path from the car park.

faqs.q: Is the East Cape drive suitable for families?
faqs.a: Yes — the East Cape circuit (SH35 from Gisborne around the cape) is one of NZ's great undiscovered road trips. The full circuit is about 330km and takes 5–6 hours driving (plan for a full day with stops). Highlights: Te Araroa (the pohutukawa tree at the beach — the oldest in NZ), Hicks Bay, and East Cape Lighthouse (the most easterly lighthouse in NZ, 700 steps up — great for kids who like a climb). Gisborne itself has excellent beaches and is the first city to see the sunrise on New Year's Day.

faqs.q: What are the best beaches in Gisborne for families?
faqs.a: Midway Beach (right in Gisborne city, patrolled in summer, excellent surf for bodyboarding), Wainui Beach (20 min north, large and less crowded), and Makorori (small, sheltered cove, excellent for swimming). Gisborne's beaches are among the least crowded in NZ — even in peak summer they're relaxed. The beach water warms up quickly here as Gisborne is the easternmost major city — swimming season starts earlier and ends later than most NZ cities.

faqs.q: Is Gisborne worth visiting as a detour?
faqs.a: Gisborne is genuinely off the main tourist circuit but rewards visitors who make the effort. Key selling points for families: excellent uncrowded beaches, the Tairawhiti Museum (free, good Māori and settler history), nearby Morere Hot Springs (natural thermal pools in native bush — excellent), and the East Cape drive. It's about 5.5 hours from Auckland via Rotorua or 6 hours from Wellington via Napier — position it as a destination rather than a quick stop.

route_gems.tagline: NZ's longest wharf (660m) jutting into the Pacific — free to walk, great fishing, and stunning bay views
route_gems.tip: Tolaga Bay is a good lunch stop on the East Cape drive. The wharf walk is popular with kids. Bring fishing rods.

route_gems.name: Mahia Peninsula

route_gems.tagline: Isolated surf beaches, dolphins, and the site of Rocket Lab's NZ launch facility — NZ's space coast
route_gems.tip: Blue Bay Beach on Mahia is superb and rarely visited. Check Rocket Lab's website for upcoming launches — you can sometimes watch from the peninsula.

name: Hamilton & Waikato
tagline: NZ's most underrated region — garden city meets rural Waikato
summary: Hamilton & Waikato is often overlooked, but it's one of NZ's best-kept family secrets. The city has world-class gardens, excellent museums, and a lovely riverside. But the real magic is the broader region: Hobbiton, horse studs, Matamata, and Waitomo Caves — all within 45 minutes. It's dramatically cheaper than Rotorua or Queenstown, genuinely family-friendly, and you'll see far fewer tourists.
verdict: Best NZ destination for families wanting Hobbiton, rural charm, and authentic NZ experiences without the crowds or high prices.
best_for: Families with kids of all ages, especially those wanting Hobbiton + rural Waikato experiences
not_ideal_for: Families who only want city attractions; weather can be wet and windy

quick_facts.must_see: Hamilton Gardens, Hobbiton, Matamata horse studs

name: Hawke's Bay
tagline: Art Deco Napier, Cape Kidnappers gannets, and NZ's best fruit — a hidden family gem
summary: Hawke's Bay is one of New Zealand's most underrated family destinations. It has Napier — the world's most complete Art Deco city — alongside Cape Kidnappers (the world's most accessible mainland gannet colony), excellent beaches, and NZ's warmest, driest climate outside Marlborough. It is cheaper, quieter, and more relaxed than the famous tourist destinations.
verdict: Excellent value, genuinely unique (the Art Deco city and gannet colony are world-class), and consistently overlooked by tourists focused on Rotorua and Queenstown. Great for families who want something different.
best_for: Families who want unique experiences away from the main tourist circuit, ages 5+
not_ideal_for: Families wanting adrenaline activities or mountain scenery

quick_facts.nearest_airport: Hawke's Bay Airport (NPE) — Air NZ from Auckland and Wellington daily

free_activities: Napier Art Deco walking — the entire CBD is a living museum of 1930s architecture, free to walk
Marine Parade — Napier's famous waterfront with sunken gardens, free fountains, skate park

hidden_gems: Cape Kidnapper tractor trek at low tide (only accessible this way, more dramatic than the walking track) — book ahead with Gannet Beach Adventures
Matahiwi Marae near Hastings — authentic Maori marae with open visits, call ahead

Ahuriri suburb in Napier — waterfront dining and cafes in a converted industrial area, much cheaper than the main Art Deco strip
Black Reef at Napier — snorkelling spot known to locals, excellent clear water on calm days
Dinosaur Museum in Hastings — small but excellent, very reasonably priced
rainy_day: Hawke's Bay Museum and Art Gallery in Napier — free, excellent Art Deco earthquake exhibition
Napier Aquarium — small but good for young kids
Hawke's Bay Opera House — check for shows and events
Indoor swimming at Splash Planet Hastings — water park, best in rain
Hastings City Library — good children's section, free

Marine Parade waterfront walk — sunken gardens, fountains, and Ocean Spa
National Aquarium of New Zealand (small but good for under-10s)
Dinner in Ahuriri suburb — better value than the tourist strip

day_2.title: Cape Kidnappers — Gannet Colony
activities: Cape Kidnappers tractor tour (book well ahead) — 10,000+ gannets nesting at the world's most accessible mainland colony
Return to Napier via Te Mata Peak (30-min detour, stunning views)
Havelock North village for lunch
Afternoon: Waimarama Beach or Ocean Beach swimming

activities: Hastings Farmers' Market (Sunday, 8:30am-12:30pm) — outstanding fresh produce and local food
Strawberry or apple picking at an Omahu Road orchard (in season)
Explore Hastings and the twin city of Havelock North

budget_breakdown.activities_per_day: $60-200 (Cape Kidnappers tour is the main paid expense)
budget_breakdown.food_per_day: $70-130 (excellent local food direct from orchards, good cafes in Napier and Havelock North)
budget_breakdown.transport_notes: 4h from Auckland, 4.5h from Wellington. Napier itself is compact and walkable. Car essential for Cape Kidnappers and beaches.

faqs.q: Is Cape Kidnappers worth visiting with young kids?
faqs.a: Yes, especially the tractor trek. Seeing 10,000+ gannets at close range is genuinely extraordinary for kids of any age. The tractor tour is accessible for ages 5+ and takes about 3 hours return. The walk (6.5km each way) is better for families with older kids (10+) who can handle the distance. Book the tractor tour well in advance — it sells out frequently in summer.

faqs.q: What is the Art Deco connection in Napier?
faqs.a: Napier was devastated by a massive earthquake in 1931 and rebuilt almost entirely in the Art Deco architectural style popular at the time. The result is one of the world's most complete and intact Art Deco cities. The self-guided walk is free and takes 1-2 hours. Children who are not interested in architecture still enjoy the elaborate facades, the old-fashioned shop fronts, and the Marine Parade waterfront.

faqs.q: Is Hawke's Bay worth visiting with kids who aren't into wine?
faqs.a: Yes — Hawke's Bay has more to offer families than just wine. Cape Kidnappers (gannet colony) is one of the most spectacular wildlife experiences in NZ. Napier's Art Deco city centre is unique and walkable. Ocean Beach is patrolled in summer and excellent. Te Mata Peak has easy walks and great views. The region is sunnier than most of NZ and the food scene is exceptional. Budget: lower than Queenstown or Rotorua.

faqs.q: What is Cape Kidnappers and is it suitable for kids?
faqs.a: Cape Kidnappers is home to one of the world's largest accessible gannet colonies — thousands of birds nest on the clifftops from October to April. You can walk (9km return from the beach car park — suitable for kids 8+ who can manage a long walk) or take a tractor tour (much easier, departs from the Gannet Beach Adventures base). The tractor tour is the family-friendly option — book ahead as it's very popular. Not accessible in winter when gannets have migrated.

faqs.q: What is the best beach in Hawke's Bay for families?
faqs.a: Ocean Beach (south of Napier) is the most popular family beach — patrolled in summer, good surf for bodyboarding, and a café nearby. Westshore Beach in Napier is very calm and suits toddlers and non-swimmers. Waimarama Beach (40 min south of Napier) is one of the most beautiful white-sand beaches in the region, uncrowded outside school holidays. The bay area has excellent rock pooling at low tide for younger explorers.

name: Marlborough & Blenheim
tagline: Sunlit sounds, wildlife cruises, and NZ's sunniest wine region — great for families
summary: Marlborough is the South Island's sunniest region and the gateway between the ferry and the rest of the South Island. Families come for the Marlborough Sounds' sheltered swimming bays, wildlife cruises, the Queen Charlotte Track, and surprisingly good food. Blenheim is a great overnight stop, and the Sounds offer some of the best kayaking for kids anywhere in NZ.

quick_facts.rainy_day_options: Limited — plan for fine weather activities
quick_facts.phone_coverage: Good in Blenheim, patchy in Sounds

top_activities: Dolphin watch cruise, Picton
Queen Charlotte Track — short sections family-friendly
Sea kayaking in the Marlborough Sounds

three_day_itinerary.day2: Pelorus Bridge swimming, Havelock mussels, Momorangi Bay evening
three_day_itinerary.day3: Blenheim, Omaka Aviation Heritage Centre, depart to Nelson or Christchurch

faqs.q: Is Marlborough good for families with young kids?
faqs.a: Yes, especially for water-confident kids 5+. The sheltered Sounds bays are perfect for swimming, kayaking, and boat trips. Momorangi Bay is ideal for toddlers — calm, warm, shallow water.

faqs.q: Is the Queen Charlotte Track suitable for families?
faqs.a: Short sections are. The Ship Cove to Resolution Bay section (2.5hr, easy) is the most popular with families. You take a water taxi out and walk back or take a water taxi both ways. Book with Cougar Line or Beachcomber Cruises in Picton.

faqs.q: Where is the best swimming in Marlborough?
faqs.a: Pelorus Bridge for river swimming (crystal clear, stunning bush setting), Momorangi Bay for sheltered Sounds sea swimming. Both are safe for children and free.

faqs.q: What is there to do in Marlborough with kids (not wine)?
faqs.a: Quite a lot — Marlborough is underrated for families beyond wine. The Marlborough Sounds by boat or kayak are spectacular (Queen Charlotte Track water taxi day trips are excellent). Havelock is the 'Greenshell Mussel Capital' — the mussel chowder at the Havelock Restaurant is worth a detour. Lake Elterwater is a quiet gem for swimming. Blenheim has the Omaka Aviation Heritage Centre (jaw-dropping WW1 aircraft — kids love it) and a good splash pad in town.

faqs.q: How far is Marlborough from Christchurch and from Wellington?
faqs.a: Marlborough (Blenheim) is about 1.5 hours south of Picton via the Queen Charlotte Drive or SH6 — beautiful coastal drive. Wellington to Picton by Interislander ferry is 3.5 hours (book ahead in summer). Christchurch is about 3.5 hours south of Blenheim via SH1 — a manageable drive with kids if you break it up at Kaikōura (whale watching, seal colony at the peninsula — worth a stop). Marlborough is ideal as a first or last stop on a South Island road trip from the ferry.

faqs.q: When should we visit the Marlborough Sounds?
faqs.a: Summer (Dec–Feb) for swimming, kayaking, and walking the Queen Charlotte Track in good conditions. Autumn (March–May) is excellent with settled weather and golden vineyards — beautiful and much quieter. Winter is mild here (Marlborough is sheltered and sunny for much of the year) and the Sounds are dramatic and uncrowded. Spring (Sept–Nov) is excellent timing before the summer crowds arrive.

route_gems.tip: Queen Charlotte Track water taxis drop you at various points for walking or kayaking. The Sounds by boat is unforgettable.

route_gems.tip: Brancott Heritage Vineyard is excellent for families (free playground, good restaurant, vineyard walk). The Marlborough Food & Wine Festival runs every February.

name: Nelson & Abel Tasman
tagline: NZ's sunniest region — golden beaches, clear water, and the most beautiful coastal walk in the world
summary: Nelson is the sunniest city in New Zealand and the gateway to Abel Tasman National Park — a coastline of golden sand beaches, crystal-clear water, and native bush. It's also one of the most relaxed and family-friendly regions in the country.
verdict: Criminally underrated by international visitors. NZ families know it — the Abel Tasman coast is spectacular and the region is brilliant for all ages.
best_for: Beach lovers, kayakers, and families who want stunning scenery without big crowds
not_ideal_for: Families wanting theme parks or urban entertainment

top_activities.name: Abel Tasman Water Taxi + Beach Day

free_activities: Tahunanui Beach — Nelson's main beach, safe swimming, playground, holiday park
Abel Tasman coastal track sections (walk in from road ends for free)
Nelson's Centre of New Zealand hill walk (20 min return, 360° views)
Rabbit Island Recreation Reserve — beautiful beach and bush
Miyazu Japanese Garden (free, peaceful)
hidden_gems: Tōtaranui Beach (Abel Tasman northern end) — the most spectacular beach in the park, accessible by road (gravel), DOC campsite on-site. Book campsites months ahead
Kaiteriteri Beach — the main Abel Tasman gateway beach is free to access and one of the best family beaches in NZ. Arrive early in summer
Mapua Wharf — 30 min from Nelson, boutique food village on the waterfront. Ice cream, fish and chips, gallery. Best evening out in the region
Golden Bay (over Takaka Hill) — worth the winding road for Pohara Beach and the spectacular Farewell Spit. Far fewer tourists
Wai-iti Lavender Farm (summer only) — free to walk around, kids love the bees and the colour
Nelson Saturday Market at Montgomery Square — the best market in NZ's South Island. Local produce, crafts, hot food, always buzzing
rainy_day: Nelson Provincial Museum (free, excellent NZ history)
World of WearableArt museum (Nelson) — $24/adult, $12/child
Founders Heritage Park with vintage train rides
Nelson's café strip on Trafalgar Street

activities: Arrive Nelson, settle in
Tahunanui Beach afternoon swim

day2.title: Abel Tasman Day
activities: Water taxi to Anchorage or Bark Bay beach
Swim, snorkel, beach lunch
Water taxi back

day3.title: Golden Bay or Kayaking
activities: Option A: Drive over Takaka Hill to Golden Bay (Pohara Beach)
Option B: Half-day guided sea kayak in Abel Tasman

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Rental car essential. Water taxis for Abel Tasman from Kaiteriteri or Marahau.

faqs.q: Do you need to walk the full Abel Tasman track?
faqs.a: No — the water taxi is the best family option. You hop on, get dropped at a beach, swim and explore, then get picked up later. No multi-day walking required.

faqs.q: Is Nelson or Kaiteriteri a better base?
faqs.a: Nelson for more amenities and a broader area; Kaiteriteri for direct Abel Tasman access. Most families base in Nelson and day-trip to Kaiteriteri (50 min drive).

faqs.q: When is Abel Tasman best for families?
faqs.a: December to March. The water is warmest (18–20°C) and all water taxis and kayak operators are fully running. October and April are quieter and still good.

faqs.q: Can you do the Abel Tasman in one day with kids?
faqs.a: Yes, with the right planning. The most popular family option is a half-day water taxi + walk combination: taxi to Anchorage or Bark Bay, walk 2–3 hours of the track, return by taxi. This avoids the full multi-day hike but gives you the coastal scenery and golden sand beaches. Book the water taxi ahead — Abel Tasman Water Taxis depart from Marahau. The kayak hire companies also do guided half-day options.

faqs.q: When is the best time to visit Nelson with kids?
faqs.a: Nelson is the sunniest city in New Zealand — it averages 2400+ sunshine hours per year. Summer (Dec–Feb) is peak for Abel Tasman, beach swimming at Tahunanui, and outdoor markets. Autumn (March–May) is excellent with settled weather and fewer people. Winter is mild and pleasant for the region (14–16°C average), making it one of NZ's best off-season destinations. Avoid Abel Tasman track in July–August if you want reliable dry conditions.

faqs.q: What are the best free activities in Nelson with kids?
faqs.a: Tahunanui Beach (patrolled in summer, playground, holiday park right on the beach), the Centre of New Zealand walk (short, 30 min return, great views), World of WearableArt Museum gardens (free outside), Nelson Saturday Market, and the Maitai Valley walks. For older kids, the Dun Mountain trail is accessible by mountain bike. The Hoglund Art Glass gallery is free and kids find the glass-blowing fascinating.

name: New Plymouth & Taranaki
tagline: Volcanic mountain, surf beaches, and NZ's best park — a hidden family gem
summary: New Plymouth is one of New Zealand's most underrated family destinations. The perfectly cone-shaped Mt Taranaki dominates the skyline, Pukekura Park is genuinely one of NZ's best parks for kids, and the coastal walkway runs 13km along dramatic black sand surf beaches. It's less crowded than Queenstown or Rotorua, more affordable, and equally beautiful.

quick_facts.phone_coverage: Good in city, limited on mountain

three_day_itinerary.day3: Brooklands Zoo, Govett-Brewster Gallery, depart south via Whanganui

faqs.q: Is New Plymouth worth visiting with kids?
faqs.a: Absolutely — it's one of NZ's best-value family destinations. Pukekura Park, the coastal walkway, Brooklands Zoo, and Govett-Brewster are all free. Accommodation is 30–40% cheaper than Queenstown or Rotorua.

faqs.q: Can kids climb Mt Taranaki?
faqs.a: The summit is for experienced adults only (technical, crampons needed in winter). Families with kids 6+ can enjoy the Dawson Falls track (1hr return, stunning waterfall), the Pouakai Tarns walk, and the visitor centre area tracks.

faqs.q: What is Pukekura Park like for families?
faqs.a: Exceptional — it's a large landscaped park with a rowing lake (hire boats for $12), a waterfall walk, a cafe, playgrounds, and the adjacent Brooklands Zoo. Plan 2–3 hours. It's completely free.

faqs.q: Is the Len Lye Centre worth visiting with kids?
faqs.a: The Len Lye Centre within the Govett-Brewster Art Gallery houses kinetic sculptures that literally move and make sound — genuinely fascinating for kids of all ages and most adults. Entry is free. Allow 1–1.5 hours. Combine it with the Pukekura Park and Brooklands Zoo which are adjacent and also free — the park has a lake with paddleboats and a fantastic children's playground.

faqs.q: What is the Coastal Walkway in New Plymouth?
faqs.a: The New Plymouth Coastal Walkway is a 13km paved path along the Taranaki coast from Fitzroy to Bell Block — one of the best urban coastal paths in NZ. Families typically do a section rather than the whole thing. The Wind Wand (a Len Lye sculpture) near the Len Lye Centre is a highlight. The walkway is flat, wide, and suitable for bikes, scooters, and prams. The section from New Plymouth CBD to Fitzroy Beach takes about 45 minutes walking.

faqs.q: Is Mt Taranaki accessible with children?
faqs.a: The lower walks around Mt Taranaki (Egmont National Park) are accessible and rewarding for families. The Enchanted Walk (30 min loop), Wilkies Pools Track (1.5 hr return to beautiful rock pools), and Kamahi Loop (1 hr) are all suitable for kids 5+. The summit is a serious mountaineering objective — do not attempt with children. Even partial ascents above the bushline require experience and equipment. Stick to the lower tracks for an excellent family experience.

hero_image.alt: Mount Taranaki volcano with snow cap above green farmland

route_gems.name: Mount Taranaki Lookout
route_gems.how_far: 30 min east of New Plymouth in Egmont National Park
route_gems.tagline: One of the world's most perfectly symmetrical volcanic cones — the summit track and short walks are spectacular
route_gems.tip: Drive to North Egmont visitor centre (free). The Pouakai Circuit (2 days) has incredible views. Short walks to Wilkies Pools and Bell Falls are family-friendly.

route_gems.how_far: In New Plymouth city centre
route_gems.tagline: Free public park with beautiful gardens, rowing lake, waterfall, and a small zoo — all completely free
route_gems.tip: Brooklands Zoo has free entry. The park is the best in NZ for a city park walk. The TSB Festival of Lights (December–February) is world-class.

route_gems.tagline: Dramatic coastal rock formations (Elephant Rock, Three Sisters) — tide-dependent walk to sea caves and arches
route_gems.tip: Check tide times — only accessible 2 hrs either side of low tide. Scrambling on rocks — wear good shoes. Totally free.

route_gems.tip: The Whanganui National Park canoe journey (3–5 days) is one of NZ's Great Walks. Day jet boat trips from Pipiriki also available.

name: Northland
tagline: Cape Reinga, Ninety Mile Beach, and ancient kauri forests — NZ's subtropical north
summary: Northland is where New Zealand begins — Cape Reinga at the very tip, Ninety Mile Beach stretching impossibly far, the Bay of Islands (its own destination), and ancient kauri trees that were old when Rome fell. It is warm, uncrowded outside peak season, and full of both Maori history and natural spectacle.
verdict: NZ's most underrated family destination. Visitors rush past Northland to get to the Bay of Islands or Rotorua — which means the rest of Northland is blissfully uncrowded.
best_for: Families who love beach walks, history, and genuinely off-the-beaten-track NZ
not_ideal_for: Families who need packed activity schedules — Northland rewards slow travel

top_activities.name: Cape Reinga Lighthouse
top_activities.price_note: Free — walking track to the tip

free_activities: Cape Reinga walkway — 30-min return to the very tip of NZ (where the Tasman Sea meets the Pacific Ocean)
Ninety Mile Beach driving (4WD only at most points, but free to walk and watch)

Pukenui wharf — a humble fishing wharf where locals catch snapper right off the dock, kids can watch or try their luck
Matauri Bay — stunning beach where the Rainbow Warrior wreck sits offshore (visible by snorkel)
Waipu Caves — glowworm cave you can self-explore with a torch, $5 entry, virtually unknown
rainy_day: Kauri Museum in Matakohe — world's best kauri collection, genuinely fascinating
Whangarei Museum and Native Bird Recovery Centre

Whangarei Town Basin — covered walkway, cafes, and the clock museum
Explore Kerikeri — charming town, great cafes, Stone Store (NZ's oldest stone building)

day_1.title: Auckland to Northland — Kauri Forest and Hokianga
activities: Drive from Auckland north (2.5h to Waipoua)
Tane Mahuta walkway — see the largest living kauri tree in NZ (free, 10-min walk)

day_2.title: Far North — Cape Reinga and Ninety Mile Beach
activities: Drive north to Kaitaia (1h 30m from Hokianga)
Optional: join a Cape Reinga day tour (includes Ninety Mile Beach sandboarding, $85-120/person)
Cape Reinga lighthouse walk — 30 minutes return, the spiritual departure point of Maori souls
Te Paki Stream — body boards down the stream outlet to the beach (bring your own or hire)

day_3.title: Doubtless Bay and Karikari Peninsula
activities: Matai Bay — double beach, protected water, perfect for young swimmers

budget_breakdown.food_per_day: $60-120 (great fish and chips, seafood direct from source)
budget_breakdown.transport_notes: 4-5h from Auckland, fuel is the main cost. Some Northland activities (Cape Reinga tours) include transport.

faqs.q: Is Cape Reinga worth the drive with kids?
faqs.a: Yes, genuinely — but it is a long day from Paihia (4h return driving) or an even longer day from Auckland. The tip of NZ where two oceans meet is a genuinely moving experience, and the Ninety Mile Beach sandboarding on the way back is a family highlight. Join an organised day tour rather than self-driving — the tour takes the beach route which is only accessible to experienced 4WD drivers.

faqs.q: What is the best base for a Northland family holiday?
faqs.a: Paihia (Bay of Islands) works for the southern Northland attractions. For the Far North (Cape Reinga, Doubtless Bay), base at Kaitaia or Mangonui. For Hokianga and Waipoua Forest, base at Opononi. A 4-5 day Northland loop starting and ending in Auckland covers all the main areas.

faqs.q: How do I get to Cape Reinga with kids?
faqs.a: Most families take a day tour from Paihia or Kaitaia — these typically include Cape Reinga, 90 Mile Beach (including optional sandboarding), and a stop at Te Paki Stream. Self-driving is possible in a regular car (Cape Reinga Road is sealed), but 90 Mile Beach is not recommended for regular vehicles — it's tidal and you can get bogged. Allow a full day from Paihia (7–8 hours total).

faqs.q: Is sandboarding at Te Paki worth it for kids?
faqs.a: Absolutely — Te Paki Stream sand dunes are a highlight that most kids rate as the best thing of the whole holiday. The dunes are massive (150m high) and you can bodyboard down on boogie boards provided by tour operators, or bring your own. The climb up is hard work in soft sand (15–20 min) but the descent is 20 seconds of pure joy. Even most 4-year-olds manage the climb with encouragement. Wear clothes you don't mind getting sandy.

faqs.q: What else is there to do in Northland besides Cape Reinga?
faqs.a: Northland is underrated beyond Cape Reinga. The Bay of Islands is the main hub (Waitangi Treaty Grounds, dolphin cruises, Russell village). Whangarei has the Town Basin, Whangarei Falls (one of NZ's most accessible waterfalls), and the Hundertwasser Art Centre. The Hokianga Harbour and giant kauri trees at Waipoua Forest (including Tāne Mahuta, NZ's largest living kauri) are genuinely special and accessible for all ages.

name: Queenstown
tagline: Adventure capital of the world — with more family fun than you'd expect
summary: Queenstown is famous for extreme adventure, but it's also one of NZ's best family destinations. The gondola and luge alone are worth the trip, the scenery is jaw-dropping, and there's enough to fill a week without spending a fortune — if you plan smart.
verdict: Best NZ destination for families with kids aged 5+. Expensive, but the experiences are world-class.
best_for: Families with kids 5 and up, especially those who love the outdoors
not_ideal_for: Toddlers or families on a tight budget
free_activities: Queenstown Gardens and frisbee golf
Lake Wakatipu foreshore walk
Frankton Beach
Fernhill Track (easy walk with lake views)
Queenstown Hill Time Walk (older kids)
rainy_day: Skyline Gondola (enclosed gondola + restaurant)
Queenstown Ice Arena
MINT Games + Activities (Queenstown CBD)
Movie at Reading Cinemas Queenstown

day1.title: Arrival + Gondola Day
activities: Arrive and check in
Skyline Gondola & Luge (afternoon)
Walk the Queenstown foreshore
Dinner at Fergburger (iconic, queues are real — go at 5pm)

activities: TSS Earnslaw Steamship cruise (morning)

Ice cream on The Mall

activities: Drive to Arrowtown (20 mins) — free gold rush historic town
Lakes District Museum (kids love the gold panning)
Drive back via Lake Hayes (iconic photo stop)
Or swap for Wanaka: Puzzling World + lake swim

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Rental car recommended. Fuel: ~$50/day if day-tripping.

faqs.q: Is Queenstown good for toddlers?
faqs.a: It's manageable but not ideal. The luge has a minimum height requirement, jet boats have minimum ages, and most adventure activities are for 5+. The gondola, foreshore, and Frankton Beach work great for under-5s.

faqs.q: What is the cheapest time to visit Queenstown?
faqs.a: April–May (autumn) is shoulder season with lower prices and stunning foliage. June–August (winter) is ski season — expensive. July–August is the peak-price period for everything.

faqs.q: Do I need to book Queenstown activities in advance?
faqs.a: Yes, especially in December–January. The Skyline and jet boats book out weeks ahead in peak season. Use Bookme for discounts.

faqs.q: How many days do I need in Queenstown with kids?
faqs.a: 3–4 days is a comfortable minimum. Day 1: Gondola and luge, lakefront walk, Queenstown Gardens. Day 2: Jet boat on the Shotover, afternoon swim at Frankton Beach. Day 3: Arrowtown day trip. Day 4: Queenstown Hill walk or adventure activity. If you have time, add a day for Wanaka (45 minutes away).

faqs.q: Is Queenstown worth visiting in winter with kids?
faqs.a: Absolutely, especially if your kids are old enough to ski (usually 3–4+ with ski school). Coronet Peak is 20 minutes from town and has a good learners' area. In winter the town is festive and far less crowded than summer. Non-skiing winter activities include the Gondola, luge (runs year-round), Underwater Observatory, and the many excellent restaurants. Accommodation is 20–30% cheaper than January.

faqs.q: What is the drive from Queenstown to Milford Sound?
faqs.a: About 4.5–5 hours each way via Te Anau — it's a long day trip with kids. Most families do it as an overnight trip (stay in Te Anau) or book a fly-cruise-fly option (fly one way, cruise, fly back) to reduce driving. The road is spectacular but involves significant winding sections — plan for car sickness with younger kids.

name: Rotorua
tagline: Geothermal wonder, Maori culture, and more activities per square km than anywhere in NZ
summary: Rotorua is arguably NZ's best pure family destination. It's affordable, centrally located, packed with activities, and the geothermal parks are genuinely unlike anything kids will have experienced. Budget around $250–400/day for a family of four.
verdict: Our #1 pick for first-time NZ family visitors. High activity density, affordable, and unforgettable.
best_for: All ages — genuinely excellent for 2-year-olds through teenagers
not_ideal_for: Those sensitive to the sulphur smell (it fades after a day)

faqs.q: Does Rotorua smell bad?
faqs.a: Yes — the sulphur (rotten egg) smell is real, especially near geothermal areas. Most visitors stop noticing after a few hours. Kids often find it funny. It's not harmful.

faqs.q: How many days do you need in Rotorua?
faqs.a: 2–3 days covers the highlights. 4 days if you want to do day trips to Wai-O-Tapu and the Hobbiton Movie Set (45 mins away).

faqs.q: Is Hobbiton worth it with kids?
faqs.a: Older kids (8+) who know Lord of the Rings love it. Under-8s may not get much from it. At $49/adult it's a significant cost — prioritise other activities for younger kids.

faqs.q: Is Rotorua smelly? Will kids complain?
faqs.a: Yes — Rotorua has a mild sulphur smell from the geothermal activity, especially in the CBD. Most kids find it funny rather than unpleasant, and you stop noticing it within a few hours. The smell is strongest near geothermal sites like Wai-O-Tapu and Hell's Gate. Hotels in the CBD can have it; those further out (e.g., near the lakes) are mostly smell-free.

faqs.q: What age is best for the Māori cultural experiences in Rotorua?
faqs.a: Ages 5 and up enjoy most cultural experiences — the kapa haka (song and dance), hangi (earth oven meal), and Māori village tours at Te Puia and Tamaki are engaging for school-age kids. Under 5s can manage but may lose focus during longer performances. Te Puia also has the famous geysers and kiwi house which younger children enjoy.

faqs.q: Can you visit Rotorua in one day from Auckland?
faqs.a: Yes — Rotorua is about 2.5 hours from Auckland. A day trip gives you enough time for Wai-O-Tapu or Te Puia, the Agrodome, and lunch. However, you'll be rushing. An overnight stay lets you do a Māori cultural evening (dinner + performance), adds Rainbow Springs and the luge, and is far less stressful. See our Rotorua day trip itinerary for a tight but doable day plan.

name: Taupo
tagline: Lake Taupo, Huka Falls, and a relaxed pace — perfect mid-island stop
summary: Taupo sits at the centre of the North Island and makes the perfect stopping point on any road trip. The Huka Falls are spectacular and free, Lake Taupo is huge and swimmable, and the town has enough to keep a family busy for 2–3 days without blowing the budget.
verdict: An essential road trip stop. Best as 2 nights between Rotorua and Wellington.
best_for: All ages, especially great for a relaxed lake holiday
not_ideal_for: Those wanting non-stop activities (it's a chill destination)
free_activities: Huka Falls viewing platform and walkway

faqs.q: Can you swim in Lake Taupo?
faqs.a: Yes — Acacia Bay and Wharewaka Point are the best family swimming spots. Clean, clear water. Cold outside of December–February.

faqs.q: Is the Spa Thermal Park really free?
faqs.a: Yes. It's a natural hot spring on the Waikato River bank, accessed via a walking track. No facilities, no fee. Bring a towel.

faqs.q: How far is Taupo from Rotorua?
faqs.a: About 1 hour by car. Most families combine both into one trip.

faqs.q: Is Huka Falls free to visit?
faqs.a: Yes — Huka Falls is completely free and one of the most spectacular sights in New Zealand. The car park is free, the walkway is flat and pram-friendly (300m to the viewing platform), and there are toilets on-site. It's the most-visited natural attraction in NZ for good reason — the volume and colour of the water is genuinely jaw-dropping.

faqs.q: Can kids swim in Lake Taupo?
faqs.a: Yes — Lake Taupo is one of the best freshwater swimming lakes in NZ. The water is clean and clear, though cold (16–20°C in summer, much colder in winter). Acacia Bay on the western shore is sheltered and popular with families. The main town beach (Two Mile Bay) also has good swimming with a playground nearby. The lake is geothermally warmed very slightly but still cool.

faqs.q: Is Taupo worth a full day or just a stop?
faqs.a: Taupo easily fills 2–3 days for families. Day 1: Huka Falls (free), Craters of the Moon walk, Taupo lakefront and beach. Day 2: Huka Jet boat, AC Baths hot pools, evening at the i-SITE foreshore. Day 3: Great Lake Trail cycling, Wairakei Thermal Valley, Aratiatia Rapids (check dam release times). It's also a natural halfway stop on the Rotorua–Wellington drive.

name: Tauranga / Mount Maunganui
tagline: NZ's most popular beach town — Mount Beach, surf, and the best fish and chips on the coast
summary: Tauranga and Mount Maunganui ('the Mount') is where New Zealand families have been going for summer holidays for generations. Mount Beach is NZ's best family beach — gentle surf, lifeguards, warm water, and excellent town amenities. It is unpretentious, warm, and has everything a family needs within walking distance.
verdict: NZ's best all-round family beach town. Not as dramatic as the Coromandel or Bay of Islands, but consistently excellent for families who want reliable beach conditions and good amenities.
best_for: Beach holidays, families with kids who want to surf or bodyboard, relaxed summer holidays
not_ideal_for: Families who need cultural or activity-heavy itineraries — it is primarily a beach destination

faqs.q: Is Mount Maunganui beach safe for young children?
faqs.a: Mount Beach has lifeguards from December to February and is generally one of NZ's safer surf beaches for families. Pilot Bay on the harbour side of the Mount is completely sheltered and perfect for toddlers. Young children who cannot swim confidently should stick to Pilot Bay or the shallow shore break at Mount Beach between the flags.

faqs.q: How far is Tauranga from Rotorua?
faqs.a: Just under 1 hour (62km). This makes Tauranga an excellent beach base with easy day-trip access to Rotorua's geothermal parks and farm shows. A 5-day trip could combine 3 nights at the Mount (beach focus) with 2 nights in Rotorua.

faqs.q: Is Mount Maunganui beach safe for young swimmers?
faqs.a: Yes — the main Mount Maunganui beach (Main Beach) is one of the safest patrolled beaches in the Bay of Plenty, with Surf Life Saving patrols operating in summer. The surf is generally moderate and the beach shelves gradually. Pilot Bay (the harbour side of the Mount) is calmer still — ideal for toddlers and non-swimmers. The Mount Hot Pools are also family-friendly with a shallow pool area for younger children.

faqs.q: Can you climb Mount Maunganui with kids?
faqs.a: Yes — the Mauao base track (flat, 3.4km around the base) is suitable for all ages and prams. The summit walk (45 min return, steep in parts) is achievable for kids 6+ who are reasonably fit. Views from the top are excellent — the Bay of Plenty coastline is spectacular on a clear day. Start early in summer to avoid the heat. The base track through the reserve passes native bush with tūī and kererū (wood pigeons).

faqs.q: What is the drive time from Auckland to Tauranga?
faqs.a: About 2.5–3 hours from Auckland via the Kaimais (SH2). The main pass road is steep and winding in places — take it slowly with young passengers. Via Hamilton (SH1 then SH29) is about the same time and flatter. Tauranga is best combined with a Coromandel Peninsula trip — you can do a loop: Auckland → Coromandel → Whitianga → Tauranga → Rotorua → Auckland in a 5–7 day road trip.

name: Wanaka
tagline: Queenstown's quieter, more beautiful neighbour — and better for families
summary: Wanaka is what Queenstown was before the crowds arrived. The lake is stunning, the town is relaxed, Puzzling World is a quirky family classic, and Treble Cone offers winter skiing for those with older kids. In summer, it's a beach town with mountains.
verdict: Better than Queenstown for a relaxed family holiday. Pair both together on a South Island trip.
best_for: Families who want scenery without Queenstown prices and crowds
not_ideal_for: Those who want maximum activity options (fewer than Queenstown)

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Car essential. Crown Range road is spectacular but steep — take it slow.

faqs.q: Is Wanaka better than Queenstown for families?
faqs.a: For young children (under 8) or families wanting a relaxed pace, yes. Wanaka is calmer, cheaper, and the lake is more accessible. For older kids wanting adventure activities, Queenstown has more options.

faqs.q: Is the Crown Range road safe to drive?
faqs.a: Yes, in good weather. It's NZ's highest paved road and spectacular. Avoid in icy conditions (winter/early spring). The alternative via Cromwell is longer but easier.

faqs.q: Is Wanaka better than Queenstown for families?
faqs.a: For younger families (under 10), many parents prefer Wanaka — it's quieter, less commercial, and the lake and town centre are more relaxed. Cardrona ski resort is world-class for families in winter. Puzzling World is a genuine Wanaka institution. The lake is spectacular for swimming and kayaking in summer. Queenstown has more activities and more restaurant variety, but Wanaka has more soul and is less rushed.

faqs.q: How far is Wanaka from Queenstown?
faqs.a: About 1 hour via the direct Crown Range Road (the highest sealed road in NZ — spectacular views but steep and winding, not suitable for campervans or in icy conditions). Via the main highway through Cromwell it's about 75 minutes. The Crown Range is worth doing in good conditions — the views from the top are extraordinary. Check conditions in winter before attempting it.

faqs.q: What is the best time to visit Wanaka with kids?
faqs.a: Summer (Dec–Feb) for lake swimming, hiking, and outdoor activities — the town is busy but manageable. Autumn (March–May) for stunning golden colours around the lake, fewer crowds, and great hiking conditions. Winter (June–Sept) for Cardrona skiing — Wanaka is much quieter than Queenstown for ski families. Spring (Oct–Nov) is underrated — warming up, lake accessible, uncrowded. Avoid peak January if you hate queues.

verdict: Best NZ city break for families — 2–3 days is perfect. Affordable, compact, world-class museum.

not_ideal_for: Beach holidays — Wellington's beaches are cold and windy

budget_breakdown.transport_notes: Compact CBD — walk or use GO Wellington buses. Cable car $12 return.

faqs.q: Is Wellington worth visiting with kids?
faqs.a: Absolutely. Te Papa alone justifies the trip — it's free, world-class, and kids can spend 3–4 hours there. Add the cable car, Zealandia, and the waterfront and you have a brilliant 2–3 day family break.

faqs.q: How windy is Wellington really?
faqs.a: Very. It earns its reputation. Always pack a windproof jacket. The wind doesn't stop you doing anything but it makes outdoor activities less pleasant in winter. Summer winds are actually refreshing in the heat.

faqs.q: Can you do Wellington as a stopover between North and South Island?
faqs.a: Yes — this is a common itinerary. Fly or drive to Wellington, spend 2 nights, then take the Interislander ferry to Picton for the South Island leg.

faqs.q: Is Te Papa free for kids?
faqs.a: Yes — Te Papa Tongarewa (the national museum) is completely free for all visitors including children. It's world-class and easily fills 3–4 hours. The natural history exhibits, the Māori collection, and the earthquake simulator are all kid-friendly highlights. It's open daily and is one of the best free family activities in New Zealand, full stop.

faqs.q: Is Wellington worth visiting with a young family?
faqs.a: Yes — Wellington has excellent family infrastructure. Te Papa is free and world-class. The cable car and Botanic Gardens are easy and scenic. Zealandia wildlife sanctuary is exceptional for native birds. The waterfront is flat, walkable, and has cafés and playgrounds. The only downside is Wellington's notorious wind — pack layers year-round. The compact city centre means you can cover a lot without much driving.

faqs.q: How many days do we need in Wellington with kids?
faqs.a: Two full days is a comfortable minimum: Day 1 — Te Papa, waterfront, cable car, Botanic Gardens. Day 2 — Zealandia, Mt Victoria lookout (for older kids), Cuba Street for lunch, Weta Workshop tour (for movie fans 10+). A third day allows for day trips — the Wairarapa wine region is 1 hour away, or Kapiti Island (world-class bird sanctuary) if booked ahead.

route_gems.how_far: 1 hr east via the Remutaka Hill Road
route_gems.tagline: NZ's pinot noir capital with child-friendly cellar doors, great food, and a classic village green
route_gems.tip: Palliser Estate and Craggy Range both welcome families. The village is extremely walkable.

route_gems.name: Kāpiti Island Bird Sanctuary
route_gems.how_far: 1.5 hrs north via Paraparaumu
route_gems.tagline: Predator-free island reserve — hear birdsong like pre-European NZ. Kiwi active at dusk.
route_gems.tip: Permit required — book months ahead at DOC. Day trips run from Paraparaumu Beach. Worth every bit of planning.

route_gems.name: Greytown
route_gems.how_far: 1 hr north-east over Remutaka Hill
route_gems.tagline: NZ's most beautiful small town — Victorian streetscape, artisan shops, excellent food, and the Cobblestones Museum
route_gems.tip: Combine with Martinborough for a great day trip. Greytown Café strip on Main Street is outstanding for brunch.

name: West Coast
tagline: Glaciers, pancake rocks, wild beaches and gold rush history — NZ's untamed side
summary: The West Coast is New Zealand's wildest and most dramatic region — and surprisingly good for families. Punakaiki's Pancake Rocks are a genuine wonder (free), Franz Josef and Fox Glaciers are accessible by short walks from the car park, and the rainforest-fringed beaches have a raw beauty you won't find anywhere else. Pack rain gear — the Coast averages 3–5 wet days per week, but weather changes fast.

faqs.q: Is the West Coast worth visiting with kids?
faqs.a: Yes — but plan around the weather. The Pancake Rocks, glaciers, and Hokitika Gorge are genuinely spectacular, and most are free. Allow extra days in your itinerary for weather delays. A campervan is ideal as it removes accommodation pressure when you need to move fast.

faqs.q: Can you still walk to the glaciers?
faqs.a: Yes — both Franz Josef and Fox have free walks to viewpoints near the glacier face. The glaciers have retreated significantly but the valley walks are still dramatic and worthwhile. Allow 1.5–2 hours return for each. Heli-hike tours are available but expensive (~$400–$500 per adult).

faqs.q: How rainy is the West Coast really?
faqs.a: It averages 3–5 rain days per week year-round. November to March is the best window. Rain tends to be heavy but short — a morning downpour often clears by lunch. Pack waterproofs and plan indoor options. The upside: when it's fine, the light is extraordinary.

faqs.q: What is Hokitika Gorge like for families?
faqs.a: The gorge is safe and walkable for all ages — 25min return on a well-formed track. The water is an extraordinary luminous turquoise from suspended glacial rock flour. The swingbridge adds excitement for kids. Go before 10am for the best light and fewer crowds.

faqs.q: Is the Pancake Rocks worth the stop with kids?
faqs.a: Absolutely — Punakaiki's Pancake Rocks and Blowholes are one of the most dramatic natural sights in NZ and the walkway is easy (20 min loop, fully paved, pram-accessible). When the swell is right, the blowholes are spectacular — check the blowhole activity schedule at the visitor centre (high swell + incoming tide is optimal). It's a perfect leg-stretch stop on the West Coast drive — allow 45 minutes including the walk.

faqs.q: Can you see the Franz Josef and Fox glaciers with kids?
faqs.a: The glacier terminals are not accessible on foot due to ongoing glacier retreat and rockfall risk — guided heli-hikes are the only way onto the glaciers themselves, starting from around $350/person (no minimum age but operators set their own limits — usually 8+ for heli-hike). However, the valley walks to the glacier viewpoints are free, scenic, and suitable for all ages. Franz Josef village also has the West Coast Wildlife Centre (kiwi and tuatara — excellent for kids, $35 adults).

faqs.q: What is the drive like along the West Coast with kids?
faqs.a: The West Coast main highway (SH6) is one of NZ's most scenic drives — stunning coastal rainforest, mountains, and wild beaches. However, it's a long drive: Greymouth to Franz Josef is 2.5 hours, Franz Josef to Haast Pass is another 2.5 hours. Plan for frequent stops and be aware that the road has limited fuel and food options between towns — fill up in Hokitika, Greymouth, and Franz Josef before remote sections. Cellphone coverage is patchy throughout.

route_gems.name: Hokitika Gorge
route_gems.how_far: 30 min east of Hokitika via Hokitika Gorge Road
route_gems.tagline: Impossibly turquoise glacial water in a narrow schist gorge — the most beautiful short walk on the West Coast
route_gems.tip: 30-min return walk on good path. Suspended swingbridge. Free. Go early morning for best colour — the blue-green is extraordinary.

route_gems.name: Pancake Rocks at Punakaiki
route_gems.how_far: 1 hr north of Greymouth on SH6
route_gems.tagline: Layered limestone formations and blowholes on the Tasman Sea — the most dramatic coastal scenery on the West Coast
route_gems.tip: 15-min loop walk from the car park. Blowholes are active at high tide — check the tide chart. Free parking and entry.

route_gems.name: Franz Josef Glacier
route_gems.how_far: 2 hrs south of Hokitika on SH6
route_gems.tagline: A glacier that ends in a rainforest — heli-hike tours or the free valley floor walk to the ice face
route_gems.tip: The free valley floor walk (1 hr return) gets you close enough to see the glacier. Heli-hikes are spectacular for older kids/teens. Guided ice walks for 8+.

route_gems.name: Westport & Charming Creek
route_gems.how_far: 2.5 hrs north of Greymouth on SH6
route_gems.tagline: The coal mining town gateway to the Denniston Plateau and the outstanding Charming Creek Walkway
route_gems.tip: Charming Creek Walkway (4 hrs, easy) follows an old coal railway through a spectacular gorge — one of NZ's most underrated family walks.
